critic_raw:
Issue 1
Claim: The six-basis-point PR-AUC gain (0.214 to 0.220) is meaningful evidence that the new model is better.
Why wrong: The baseline was evaluated on Q1 with 30-day provisional fraud labels, while the challenger was evaluated on Q2 with 90-day matured labels. These are not the same target. 90-day matured labels capture more fraud cases (late chargebacks, extended dispute resolution) than 30-day provisional labels. A model evaluated against a richer, more complete target will tend to appear better than one evaluated against an incomplete target, even if the models have identical underlying quality. The six-basis-point gain may be entirely attributable to label maturation, not model quality.
Evidence: Re-evaluate both models on the same quarter with the same 90-day matured labels and recompute the PR-AUC comparison.

Issue 2
Claim: The Q1 and Q2 evaluation windows can be compared fairly.
Why wrong: A merchant onboarding campaign between Q1 and Q2 changed the mix of transaction sizes and geographies. The Q2 population that the challenger was evaluated on is structurally different from the Q1 population the baseline was evaluated on. Population shift between evaluation windows confounds model quality with data distribution change — the challenger may look better simply because Q2's transaction mix happens to align better with the challenger's feature set.
Evidence: Re-evaluate both models on the same quarter (ideally using the Q2 period with mature labels for both). A stable test window eliminates distribution shift as a confound.

Issue 3
Claim: A six-basis-point absolute gain is operationally meaningful and justifies migration.
Why wrong: PR-AUC for rare-class fraud is inherently noisy. Without a confidence interval or paired significance test, there is no way to determine whether the 0.006 PR-AUC difference is a reliable signal or random variation. At 0.2% prevalence, PR-AUC estimates can have substantial variance across evaluation windows, and a difference this small may be within the noise floor of the metric at typical fraud dataset sizes.
Evidence: Compute paired bootstrap confidence intervals for the PR-AUC difference using the same evaluation window and label standard. Report whether the 95% CI excludes zero.
defender_raw: The temporal splits by week are a sound design choice for fraud — random splits would create look-ahead bias in fraud detection. The model shows improvement in PR-AUC. The fraud operations team considered a six-basis-point improvement operationally meaningful given the investigation workload impact. The 90-day maturation window was chosen for the challenger because a more complete label set is available and more reliable. There is nothing inherently wrong with using better labels for the newer model when those labels are available.
adjudication_raw: The defender's response fails on all three points. On Issue 1: the defender says 90-day labels are 'better' and defends using them for the challenger — but the point is that the comparison is unfair when the baseline uses 30-day labels. A fair comparison requires the same label standard for both models. On Issue 2: the defender does not address the population shift from the merchant onboarding campaign. On Issue 3: the defender notes the fraud team considered the gain 'operationally meaningful' but does not address the absence of statistical uncertainty — at 0.2% prevalence, this is not optional. All three issues are valid and unaddressed by the defense. Verdict: critique_wins.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: label maturation mismatch — baseline 30-day vs. challenger 90-day labels
Issue 2: different evaluation quarters with changed transaction mix from merchant onboarding campaign
Issue 3: no confidence interval for a six-basis-point gain at rare fraud prevalence

empirical_test.condition: Re-evaluate both baseline and challenger on the same quarter with the same 90-day matured labels; compute paired bootstrap 95% CI for the PR-AUC difference
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Common-window, common-label evaluation shows PR-AUC gap under 0.003 or the paired bootstrap 95% CI crosses zero
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Common-window evaluation shows PR-AUC gap of at least 0.006 with 95% CI excluding zero
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Gap is positive but below 0.006 and the CI barely overlaps zero
